era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues into its third year. The plague has subsided in Athens, but its demographic and political impact persists. Pericles dies in 429, marking a generational shift in Athenian leadership. Cleon and other demagogues rise in influence. Sparta maintains its land dominance; Athens relies on naval superiority and its empire. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final decades; regional consolidation accelerates.

events.title: Death of Pericles
events.description: Pericles, the dominant Athenian statesman for three decades, dies in 429 BCE, likely from the plague or a plague-related illness. His death marks the end of an era of Athenian cultural and political leadership. Plutarch reports that Pericles' death occurred in the autumn of 429, after the plague had begun to subside. His passing removes the architect of Athenian imperial strategy and opens the way for more aggressive, populist leaders like Cleon. The loss is both symbolic and practical: Pericles' prestige and strategic vision had held together the Athenian coalition.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II, 65

events.certainty_note: Thucydides and Plutarch both attest to Pericles' death in 429. The exact cause (plague vs. other illness) is less certain, but the year is secure.

events.title: Third Spartan invasion of Attica
events.description: King Archidamus II leads the third annual Spartan invasion of Attica. As in previous years, the Spartans ravage the countryside but fail to draw the Athenians into a pitched battle. The invasion achieves no strategic breakthrough; Spartan strategy of attrition is proving ineffective against Athenian naval resources and the Long Walls. The campaign reinforces the stalemate that will characterize the first decade of the war.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II, 71

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents the invasion as part of the annual cycle of Spartan campaigns.

events.title: Phormio's naval victories in the Corinthian Gulf
events.description: The Athenian admiral Phormio achieves two significant naval victories against Corinthian and allied fleets in the Corinthian Gulf. In the first engagement (off Patrae), Phormio's 20 ships defeat a larger Corinthian fleet. In the second (off Rhium), Phormio again defeats a combined Peloponnesian fleet. These victories demonstrate Athenian naval superiority and boost morale after the plague and Pericles' death. Phormio becomes a celebrated figure in Athens, embodying the promise of naval strategy.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II, 83–92

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides detailed accounts of both engagements, including tactical details. The victories are well-attested.

events.title: Siege of Potidaea concludes; Athenian victory
events.description: After a grueling siege lasting from 432 to 429 BCE (approximately 28 months), Potidaea surrenders to the Athenians. The city's population is expelled, and Athenian colonists are settled there. The siege has been costly for Athens in lives and resources, particularly due to disease among the besieging forces. However, the victory secures Athenian control of Chalcidice and eliminates a major Corinthian foothold in the north. The siege becomes a symbol of Athenian determination but also of the war's grinding attrition.
key_figures: Xenophon (Athenian general, involved in final assault)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II, 70

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides the date and outcome of the siege's conclusion.

events.title: Athenian expedition to Acarnania and Aetolia
events.description: Athens dispatches a naval and land force under Phormio to secure allies in western Greece and disrupt Corinthian trade routes. The campaign achieves mixed results; Phormio secures Acarnania as an Athenian ally, but an attempt to invade Aetolia fails due to the Aetolians' light-armed tactics and difficult terrain. The expedition reflects Athens' strategy of using naval mobility to project power beyond the Aegean.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II, 102–103

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents the campaign; the precise dating to 429 is based on his chronological framework, which is generally reliable but not always precise to the year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Pericles' death is given as 429 by Thucydides and Plutarch, but some modern scholars have questioned whether it occurred in 429 or 430, or even whether the plague was the direct cause. The cause of death is not explicitly stated in primary sources; later sources infer plague-related illness. Additionally, the dating of Phormio's naval victories to 429 is based on Thucydides' chronological framework, which some scholars have questioned as potentially compressed or rearranged for narrative effect.
historiographic_note: The record for 429 BCE remains rich, dominated by Thucydides' account. However, Thucydides' narrative is shaped by Athenian perspective and his own political views. The death of Pericles is treated as a watershed moment, which may reflect Athenian literary tradition more than objective historical significance. Spartan and Corinthian perspectives are largely absent. The siege of Potidaea is presented as a triumph, but the human cost and the strategic value of the victory are debated by modern historians.

graph_edges.note: Pericles' death opens the way for Cleon's rise to prominence in Athenian politics.
Phormio's naval victories establish Athenian naval dominance, which continues to shape strategy in subsequent years.
Conclusion of the siege that began in 432.
